query: definition and classification of anxiolysis according to ASA guidelines

retrieved_docs.title: American Society of Anesthesiologists Staging -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1963, the American Society of Anesthesiologists instituted a system to assess a patient's physical health status and clinical risk during anesthetic administration and surgical operation. Pre-operatively, the patient is subjectively assigned a score according to their physical status, which is determined by the anesthesiologist after considering the patient's presentation, history, and functional limitations. Assigning this score, ranked ASA I through ASA VI, would thereby attempt to categorize the patient's risk of perioperative complications based on their physical status and overall health. Patients assigned to higher numerical categories have an increased risk of perioperative adverse events. The goal of creating the ASA Physical Status Classification System (ASA-PS) was to improve patient outcomes and predict perioperative risk. [1] Despite its setbacks, it has since become a standard practice during perioperative encounters and plays a key role in preventative medicine associated with anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Open Airway Procedural Sedation -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no universally accepted guidelines that outline strict/absolute contraindications for OAPS, as many life-saving procedures need to be performed on patients regardless of their underlying comorbidities. Nonetheless, a thorough and pertinent history and physical exam should be performed on every patient to assess the patient's overall perioperative health. The ASA Physical Status Classification System, created in 1941, is a valuable grading system that is used to evaluate and communicate a patient's level of overall perioperative health before surgery and the administration of anesthesia:

retrieved_docs.title: American Society of Anesthesiologists Staging -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Using the ASA Physical Status Classification System to evaluate and prepare for possible adverse events remains 1 of the most widely used pre-operative screening methods for all providers worldwide [5] . The ASA Physical Status Classification System has been shown to predict the frequency of perioperative morbidity and mortality. Research has shown that the implementation of the classification system correctly predicts the frequency and severity of adverse events, which improves patient outcomes [5] . The ASA score is assigned based on a patient's presence and severity of systemic disease. Examples of some disease processes commonly encountered are listed with their respective score assignment. The letter E may be added to any category (ie, ASA IIE) to denote an emergency.

retrieved_docs.title: American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification System -- Other Issues -- Examples of the American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification System
retrieved_docs.content: Patient 6: A 25-year-old man sustained a severe head and cervical spine injury in a motorcycle accident while not wearing a helmet. Despite surgical decompression and aggressive management of his intracranial process, he remains unresponsive to all noxious stimulation during his intensive care unit stay. Testing for brain death is carried out according to the American Academy of Neurology guidelines for Brain Death Determination, which reveals a complete absence of central nervous system function. His family agrees to make his organs available for transplantation. This patient is classified as ASA class 6. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: American Society of Anesthesiologists Physical Status Classification System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The ASA Physical Status Classification System is a numeric score assigned to a particular patient based on the extent of systemic disease as assessed through the patient's medical history and the extent of the patient's function limitation. When used as intended, the scoring system provides a general conceptual approach to perioperative risk assessment and may be useful to individualize management of surgical patients. [9] However, it does not necessarily correlate with overall risk because other factors (as mentioned above) also contribute to perioperative risk. For example, the operative risk for a patient with ASA Class IV undergoing cataract surgery under topical anesthesia is different than that for the same patient undergoing an esophagectomy. Likewise, a patient with ASA Class IV due to severe underlying disease burden who walks 500 meters daily is different than the same underlying disease burden who is wheelchair-bound. Finally, a healthy patient (ASA Class I) without any physical or external contraints is different than a healthy patient with a known difficult airway or orthodox Jehovah's Witnesses who refuses blood and blood products. In these examples, the ASA physical status classification system score may be the same, but the patient's overall operative risk is not. [9] [3] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified as a specific type of anxiety disorders and is defined by particular diagnostic criteria that guide its identification and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1576
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders comprise a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. One such subtype, Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, is classified under the related category Anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition and is defined by explicit diagnostic criteria that are incorporated into the overall diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Another distinct entity, Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere, is recognized as a child category within the anxiety disorders hierarchy. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and the associated diagnostic criteria is essential for accurate identification and appropriate management of patients whose anxiety symptoms are linked to underlying medical or physiological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is classified within anxiety disorders and adheres to the diagnostic criteria established for anxiety disorders. Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is also situated under the anxiety disorders category, identified as a subcategory (has parent) of anxiety disorders. Both entities illustrate how specific mental health conditions are organized under the broader anxiety disorders classification, emphasizing the role of diagnostic criteria and hierarchical relationships in accurate diagnosis and patient care.

The broader anxiolytic category (“anxiolytika”) is linked to beta‑blocking agents, reflecting occasional use of β‑blockers for anxiety. Collectively, beta‑blocking agents share β‑adrenergic antagonism and have overlapping applications across multiple therapeutic areas, with defined contraindications and combination strategies that guide clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is designated for use in the prevention and control of several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, it applies to the prevention and management of التهاب وريدي (vascular inflammation), انسداد الحالب (ureteral obstruction), and اضطراب قلق الانفصال (separation anxiety disorder). By employing the allowed qualifier, clinicians can implement strategies aimed at reducing the incidence, mitigating the severity, and controlling the progression of these diverse disorders, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care across inflammatory, obstructive, and psychiatric domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.
